topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the screen, his knuckles white where he gripped the mouse. The carefully crafted prose, the intricate character arcs he had poured his very soul into—it was all there, subtly shifted, undeniably stolen. A cold, sharp feeling tightened in his chest, constricting his breath. He reread the passages, his eyes scanning for the telltale echoes, the unmistakable fingerprint of his own imagination twisted into something hollow. When he finally found the direct parallels, a slow, simmering fury began to bubble beneath his skin. He slammed his laptop shut, the sound echoing unnervingly loud in the quiet study. "This is a betrayal," he muttered, his voice tight, the air around him suddenly thick with unspoken accusation. He stood up, pacing the length of his desk, his movements jerky and agitated, unable to settle until the injustice of it was confronted.

[story 2]

"You took my words," Clara said, the words clipped, each syllable laced with a brittle tension. Her hands trembled slightly as she held the printed manuscript, the pages feeling alien and tainted in her grasp. She looked up at the editor, her gaze unwavering, challenging. "Every pivotal scene, every unique dialogue exchange—it’s lifted wholesale. I spent years building that world, brick by painstaking brick, and someone else just... copied the blueprint. A deep flush crept up her neck, and she clenched her jaw so hard a muscle jumped beneath her skin. She didn't raise her voice, but the quiet intensity of her disappointment was far more potent than any shout. She turned sharply, walking toward the door, her steps quick and purposeful, needing to escape the suffocating atmosphere of the room.

[story 3]

"I can't believe this," Marcus whispered, running a hand roughly through his already disheveled hair. The sheer audacity of it made his stomach churn. He felt a hot wave wash over him, starting in his core and spreading outward, making his palms sweat. He leaned back in his chair, his posture rigid, his eyes fixed on the offending text on the monitor. He let out a low, guttural sound—a sound that was more animal than human—and then he began to methodically delete the plagiarized sections, his fingers flying across the keyboard with a furious, almost violent speed. Every keystroke was a declaration, a physical manifestation of the deep-seated feeling of being profoundly wronged. He stopped only when the screen was clean, breathing heavily, the silence now charged with residual tension.
